turns.content: I'm trying to write a utility crate that provides a macro, but when I put the definition at the top level of my crate and try to use it in another crate by just adding `use` statements, it fails. It seems like the macro isn't actually exported for external crates to see.

That is a very common stumbling block when moving from simple macros to ones you want to share. The core issue here is that standard `use` statements only work if the macro was defined with `#[macro_export]`. Without this attribute, the compiler doesn't register the token as being available for re-export or import in other crates.
So I need to add that specific attribute to my macro definition? But why does it make such a big difference compared to just having the code there?

Exactly. The `#[macro_export]` attribute is the mechanism Rust uses to tell the compiler that this token should be placed in the macro table and made accessible globally within your library's namespace. Without it, the macro remains local to its definition file.
Got it. So if I add `#[macro_export]` at the top of my crate where I define the macro, then other crates can just do a normal import like `use your_crate::your_macro_name!`. Is that right?

Yes, you have it exactly right. Once you annotate the definition with that attribute, the macro becomes part of the public interface of your crate and can be imported using standard path syntax in downstream crates.
What about older versions before 2018? I remember seeing `#[macro_use]` mentioned somewhere on some old docs. Is that still relevant?

The `#[macro_use]` attribute is part of the pre-2018 syntax and was used with extern crates to import macros into the current scope. It has been completely removed in modern Rust because the `use` statement approach combined with `#[macro_export]` provides better clarity and control.

That makes sense. I was looking at some old migration guides and got confused by that legacy syntax. Is there any scenario where re-exporting a macro from one crate to another requires anything special beyond just the normal import?

For simple cases, no. You can just `use` it directly. However, if you want your utility crate to act as a bridge and re-export macros from yet another library, you would define those macros inside your crate with the export attribute yourself.
Wait, so I can't just pass through an import? Like `use std::env` for normal items but maybe not for macros?
You cannot simply re-export a macro from another crate using a plain `use`. If you want to expose that macro, your intermediate crate must define its own version with the `#[macro_export]` attribute.

Ah! So if I write a wrapper crate, I have to duplicate the macro definition but just change the name and add that export tag. That's why my first attempt failed—I was trying to import instead of defining it locally.

That is precisely the misunderstanding you encountered. To make a macro available externally, your crate must define it itself using `#[macro_export]`, and then users import that definition rather than trying to pull in an external one.

I keep thinking this is just like importing a trait or a function, but when I try to use a macro from another crate in my main application it fails with an undefined reference error even though the macro definition exists. It feels like macros are somehow more opaque than regular functions because they get expanded at compile time rather than called at runtime.

You have hit on exactly why this is so frustrating for porters from other languages. Unlike a trait or function where you just call `trait_name::method()`, a macro definition needs to be visible in the compiler's symbol table before any expansion happens, and if you try to use it without proper export rules, the compiler simply cannot see the tokens needed to expand your code. The core distinction is that macros are not just functions; they are syntactic sugar generators that require specific visibility markers to cross crate boundaries effectively.

So I'm confused about whether I need #[macro_export] on every macro definition or if there's a simpler way like just importing it directly? And why did the old #[macro_use] attribute even exist before 2018?

In modern Rust with edition 2018 and later, you never use #[macro_use]; that directive was deprecated early on. Instead of relying on global imports that affect the entire crate namespace like extern crate used to, you must explicitly place #[macro_export] on the macro definition itself at the very top level of its defining module or crate root. This tells the compiler that this macro is intended for public consumption and should be available anywhere in your project's import graph without needing special imports from other crates.
Okay so if I define a macro in my library crate, do I need #[macro_export] there? And then how does it actually get into my application crate without any extra work?

Yes, you absolutely need #[macro_export] on the definition side inside your library crate to make it visible externally. Once that attribute is present on the macro definition, the macro becomes part of your public API automatically. In your application crate, you don't need any special import syntax like `use lib::Macro` or similar; instead, you simply write the macro invocation directly as if it were defined locally in your current file, and the compiler will resolve it to the exported version from your library.

That sounds convenient but I'm worried about re-exporting. If I have a crate that provides multiple macros, how do I ensure they are all accessible downstream without polluting the namespace? Can't I just import them individually like traits?

You can absolutely re-export individual macros using `pub use` statements within your own module or at the crate root level to control exactly which macros are exposed. For example, you might define several helper macros internally and then only `pub use my_helper_macro!` in your public API surface. This approach gives you fine-grained control similar to how traits can be re-exported selectively via `use` paths, but for macros specifically, the mechanism relies on explicit re-exports rather than automatic global availability.

I see. So if I'm building a crate that other crates depend on, should all my helper macros have #[macro_export] or just the ones I want to be public? And what happens if someone tries to use an internal macro without proper export?

Only the macros you intend to make publicly available need #[macro_export]. Internal helper macros that are only useful within your own crate should remain private and omit this attribute entirely. If a downstream consumer attempts to invoke such an unexported macro, they will receive a hard error indicating that the macro is not defined in their current scope, which serves as a clear signal that either the macro wasn't intended for external use or it requires proper re-exporting before consumption.

This distinction between exported and internal macros feels like something I've never encountered cleanly in other languages. Is there any risk of accidentally exposing too many macros? And how does the compiler actually validate these exports during the expansion phase?

The main risk is forgetting #[macro_export] on a macro you thought should be public, which leads to cryptic compilation errors when external crates try to use it. The compiler validates this by checking whether every macro invocation has a corresponding definition in its current scope or an exported one in the same crate hierarchy. If you attempt to expand a macro that lacks #[macro_export] from the perspective of another crate, the symbol resolution fails because the macro tokens aren't registered globally, resulting in undefined reference errors rather than warnings.

It seems like macros are fundamentally different from functions because they don't have signatures or types. Can I still document them properly? And what if I want to provide a default implementation for a generic macro that other crates can customize?

While macros lack traditional type signatures, you can still document their intended usage via inline comments and godoc-style annotations within the macro definition itself before any attributes. For providing customizable defaults across crates, you define your base macro with #[macro_export] in a shared library crate, then allow downstream crates to override specific parts by re-exporting their own versions or using conditional compilation traits if needed, though pure macro customization often requires careful design since macros can't be polymorphic in the same way functions are.

I'm still struggling with the idea that #[macro_export] makes a macro available everywhere. Does this mean I can use it anywhere even without importing anything? And what if I want to restrict usage to specific modules only?

With #[macro_export], the macro becomes globally visible within its defining crate, so you don't need explicit imports to invoke it from other files in that same crate. However, restricting access is still possible by placing the macro definition inside a private module or using visibility modifiers like `pub(crate)` on the containing module while keeping the macro itself public only for external crates if desired. This allows granular control similar to how traits can be restricted to specific visibility levels without breaking the export mechanism.

Okay so I think I understand now. But what about macros that need to interact with standard library types? Like using std::mem::replace in my macro definition, does #[macro_export] still work correctly?

Yes, #[macro_export] works perfectly fine even when your macro references or expands into expressions involving standard library items like std::mem::replace. The macro itself remains a local construct within its defining crate, but because of the export attribute, it can be invoked externally while still referencing stable library components internally. The compiler resolves these internal dependencies automatically as long as they are part of the standard library or properly re-exported in your crate.

Great! One last question though. If I'm migrating an old project that used #[macro_use] with extern crate, how do I translate that legacy pattern into modern syntax? Do I need to rewrite all those macros?

You don't necessarily need to rewrite every macro if you can simply add #[macro_export] to their definitions and remove the #[macro_use] attributes entirely. The transition involves converting the old extern crate + #[macro_use] pattern into modern #[macro_export] declarations at the appropriate module boundaries, ensuring that previously globally available macros are now properly exported for reuse without relying on deprecated import mechanisms.
I've been reading about macros in Rust, but I'm still not quite sure how to export a macro from one crate to another. Can you explain?
Sure! The way you export macros across crates depends on the version of Rust you're using. Before 2018 edition, you'd use `#[macro_use]` on an external crate that exports macros. For example:
Oh wait, I thought macro_export was just for defining macros inside a crate? How does it relate to exporting macros?

Good question! `#[macro_export]` is used at the crate root when defining a macro. It makes that macro available to other crates importing this one. So, if you define your macro in lib.rs and add `#[macro_export]`, then other crates can use it. This replaces the older `#[macro_use] extern crate foo;` approach which was required before 2018 edition.
So, if I define a macro in lib.rs and mark it with #[macro_export], other crates can just use it directly? That sounds simple!
Exactly! You don't need to do anything special. The user will simply write `use crate_name::macro_name!` or call the macro by its name if it's in the root namespace.
But wait, I thought macros were meant to be local? Why would we want to export them?

Exporting macros is useful when you want to share common utilities or abstractions across crates. Imagine a crate for logging or error handling; if it exports its macros, downstream projects can use those macros consistently without duplicating code.
Okay, so in 2018 edition and later, is there any difference?

Yes! Starting from 2018 edition, you don't need `#[macro_use]` anymore. Instead, just define your macros with `#[macro_export]`, and users can import them directly using `use crate_name::macro_name!`. This is cleaner and more explicit.
That makes sense. So if I have a macro in my library, other crates just need to write `use crate_name::my_macro!`?

Yes! That's the easiest way. But sometimes you might want to re-export macros from one crate into another, especially if your crate is a dependency management tool for other crates. You can do this with `pub use crate_name::macro_name;` inside your own lib.rs.
Got it. So, in summary: define macros with #[macro_export], export them from your library root (or re-export), and import with `use crate_name::macro_name!`. That's straightforward!
Exactly! And remember, this approach reduces boilerplate and promotes code reuse. If you have any more questions about macros or specific examples, just ask.
